observations.recommendation:
1.8. We recommended and management agreed to:

a. direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to make necessary adjustments in the accounting books and cashbooks to reflect the correct ending balances of the affected Cash in Bank accounts and forward adjusted beginning balances; and

b. henceforth, record transactions based on the actual occurrence and substance and always present the financial statements fairly in all materials respects.

2.12. In view of the deficiencies cited above, we recommended and management agreed to:

a. require the Property Custodian to maintain an updated Property Acknowledgement Receipt (PAR) and ensure that transfer of custody of assets from one person to another should be properly documented. Furthermore, the Municipal Accountant shall maintain an updated PPELC and Lapsing Schedule to be submitted to the audit team along with the Report on Physical Count of PPE (RPCPPE) to facilitate the accuracy and reliability of PPE accounts; and

b. create an Appraisal and Disposal Committee to fast track the appraisal of the unserviceable properties totaling P6.450 million and dispose the same to avoid further deterioration of the said assets in accordance with to Section 79 of P.D. No. 1445.

3.13. We recommended and Management agreed to:

a. Direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to intensify their collection efforts and issue demand letters to concerned NGOs/POs and individuals who have outstanding past due accounts as of year-end;

b. direct the Municipal Accountant to conduct an assessment of the collectability of the dormant and impaired receivable accounts totaling to P368,554.09 and request write-off according to the procedures provided under COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016; and

c. henceforth, prepare and maintain subsidiary ledgers for all receivables in accordance with Section 4(e) of the MNGAS for LGUs.

4.6. We recommended and management agreed to:

a. initiate the reconciliation of accounts with the Office of the President and confirm if a reciprocal Due from account is recorded in their books. Furthermore, request a copy of MOA and submit to the audit team for verification; and

b. submit to the audit team and Office of the President the DVs and other supporting documents for post-audit and liquidation of the fund transfer. Otherwise, the unliquidated amount of one million pesos shall be refunded to the source agency or to the Bureau of Treasury.

5.6. We recommended and management agreed to:

a. instruct the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that all collections are deposited intact and in required frequency as mandated by Item 33, Chapter 2 of the Revised Cash Examination Manual to prevent the exposure of cash to theft and misappropriation; and

b. direct the Municipal Treasurer to deposit immediately the accumulated undeposited collections totaling ₱220,846.67 and forfeit the over-deposit of P5,742.80 in favor of the government and recognize a Miscellaneous Income if reason of overage cannot be satisfactorily traced and justified.

6.9. We recommended and Management agreed to:

a. regulate the construction of buildings and other structures within the Municipality for the safety of its constituents and generate additional income by directing the Municipal Treasurer with the assistance of the Municipal Engineer to impose the collection of building permit fees in compliance with Sections 208 and 301 of the IRR of PD No. 1096; and

b. direct the Municipal Treasurer to dispose the income to be generated from the building permit fees and other charges in accordance with DPWH and DILG JMC No. 001 dated July 4, 2013.

7.6. We recommended and management agreed to submit a written justification explaining the reason of the late submission of contract documents and henceforth, submit copies of duly perfected contracts within five working days from perfection thereof to the Auditor for prompt review and evaluation as required under COA Circular No. 2009-001.

8.6. We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to ensure that collections and deposits for the Cash in Treasury account are recorded and updated on the cashbook on a daily basis based on the RCDs to facilitate timely monitoring of cash on hand balances in adherence to Section 26, Volume II of the MNGAS for LGUs.

9.5. We recommended and management agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer to request the transfer of the unexpended DRRMF for the current year totaling P3,917,544.30 from GF to the depository account for Trust Liability-DRRM fund and henceforth, comply with the regulations on the transfer of unexpended/unobligated balance of the LDRRMF to the Trust Fund.

10.6. We commended Management on its strict implementation on the allocation of 20 percent of the total IRA and implementation of development projects for the current year. However, we recommended and Management agreed to also monitor the implementation of projects from its continuing appropriations of Development Fund to further promote optimal quality delivery of services to its constituents.

13.7. We recommended and Management agreed to:
• instruct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to initiate a reconciliation with the GSIS to trace details of the beginning balance amounting to P3,957.08 and remit the said balances to GSIS and/or adjust accounting book balances accordingly;
• instruct the Municipal Treasurer to remit the unremitted withholdings for CY 2024 amounting of P3,439.06 to the GSIS; and
• direct both the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, to henceforth, maintain a reconciled Due to GSIS accounts and ensure that all withholdings are remitted intact and within the period prescribed under RA No. 8291.

14.6. We recommended and Management agreed to:
• direct the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to initiate a reconciliation with the HDMF Office to trace the details of the P8,543.56 and the over remittance of P100.00 and remit the net balance and/or adjust accounting balances accordingly;
• direct the Municipal Treasurer to remit the payment for loan amounting to P1,108.70; and
• direct both the Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer, to henceforth, maintain a reconciled Due to GSIS accounts and ensure that all withholdings are remitted intact and within the period prescribed under RA No. 9679.
15.5. We recommended and Management agreed to remit the amount P579.00 to PhilHealth and henceforth, ensure that withholdings are remitted intact and within the period prescribed under RA No. 10606.

1.12. We recommended and the Management agreed to:
a. require the Municipal Accountant and the GSO to (i) maintain
subsidiary records for PPE items such as PPE ledger cards and property
cards, respectively, starting with current year acquisitions until all items
are covered; and (ii) reconcile the respective records of the Accounting
Office and Property Officer on PPE; and
b. direct all concerned municipal officials to make concerted efforts to
reproduce titles for all lots owned by the Municipality and to duly
recognize these in the municipal land records using appraised values.

2.6. We recommended and the Management agreed to require the property office
to maintain stock cards to (1) establish adequate controls over the receipt, issuance,
transfer, and disposal of semi-expendable property, and (2) facilitate the
reconciliation of property and accounting records.

3.8. We commended and recommended that the municipality continue its
exemplary efforts of increasing its revenue collection on business taxes in pursuit of
its objectives of providing essential social services for the welfare of its residents.

4.8. We recommended that the:
a. Municipal Treasurer to post notice of delinquency for property
taxes at the main entrance of the municipal hall building and in
publicly accessible and conspicuous place in each barangay where
these properties are located; seek assistance of the concerned
barangays for the issuance of Notice of Delinquency and conduct an
information dissemination regarding the importance of paying taxes
promptly and the consequences of non-payment of taxes; and if
warranted, enforce the provision of Section 254 (a) and (b) RA 7160
in the collection of delinquent taxes; and
b. Municipal Accountant reconciles with the office of the Municipal
Treasurer the variance noted.

4.9. According to management, they understand the concern regarding the growing
amount of delinquent RPT accounts. Significant measures have already been initiated to
address this concern, to wit:
a. Barangay-Level RPT Information and Education Campaign (IEC):
In collaboration with the Assessor’s Office and as directed by the
Municipal Mayor, the Municipal Treasurer’s Office launched an RPT
IEC drive at the barangay level. Personnel were deployed to conduct
house-to-house visits, delivering delinquency notices and educating
taxpayers on their obligations under the Local Government Code (RA
7160), particularly on timely payment of real property taxes;
b. Posting of Delinquent Properties:
In compliance with Sec. 254 of RA 7160, notices of delinquent
properties were posted on Barangay bulletin boards, making information
accessible to the public and helping foster transparency;
c. Identification of Double-Declared Properties:
Through the said PPA, several double-declared properties have been
identified which significantly contributed to the unreconciled and
inflated RPT Receivable records. These findings will aid in the on-
going data cleansing efforts;
d. Request for Exclusion of Tax-Exempt Properties:
The Acting Municipal Treasurer has formally requested the review and
possible exclusion of public schools and government-owned properties
erroneously listed as taxable, which constitute a significant portion of
the total delinquency amount, and
e. Challenges Encountered:
Despite these efforts, the office continues to face operational challenges,
such as (a) Lack of personnel, limiting the coverage of house-to-house
campaigns and (b) Data cleansing issues, particularly in relation to
double declarations and tax exempt properties being erroneously listed
as taxable.

4.10. Furthermore, the LGU remains committed to the implementation of the
recommendations and necessary actions are being taken to continue the
reconciliation of Accounting and Treasurer’s records, intensify RPT collection
drives and monitoring, strengthen coordination with the Assessor’s Office and
barangays, facilitate accurate and updated RPT records, and ensure full compliance
with the provisions of the Local Government Code.

5.14. However, we still recommended and the Local Chief Executive, Sangguniang
Bayan and Local Development Council agreed to continue complying with the above
regulations and to continue (a) investing in high-impact capital expenditure projects
in the utilization of its 20% Development Fund and (b) ensuring the effective and
efficient implementation of the proposed projects within their targeted completion
period through close monitoring and supervision to maximize the developmental
benefits and the early enjoyment thereof by the intended beneficiaries.

6.5. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive and heads of concerned
implementing offices agreed to:
(a) implement without further delay the remaining projects not
completed/implemented in CY 2024;
(b) continue to monitor and supervise the effective and efficient
implementation of projects funded from various source agencies
within the timelines in the MOA; and
(c) continue to be prompt in the return of excess funds to the source
agencies upon completion or termination of the project.

7.13. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the
LDRRM Officer to (i) include under a separate caption, the list of projects and
activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in the LDRRMIFP;
and (ii) continue utilizing the fund for the implementation of projects and activities
for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response and rehabilitation and
recovery.

8.20. However, we recommended and the Municipal Solid Waste Management
Board, through the SWM Focal Person agreed to:
(a) establish MRFs in the 10 barangays to facilitate the segregation of
solid waste at the barangay level;
(b) coordinate efforts with the barangay officials on the implementation
of the five basic environmental laws on preserving and protecting the
environment required under R.A. No. 9003 and require all barangays
located in the coastal areas to have proper solid waste management
disposal and closely monitor their implementation;
(c) instruct the barangay officials to instruct their barangay
collectors/segregators/ sorters to properly sort the wastes they had
collected before transferring them to the Central MRF; and
(d) strictly enforce the Sangguniang Bayan ordinance on the “no
segregation, no collection” policy.

9.7. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive, as Chairman of the LSB,
agreed to (a) instruct the LSB to the DepEd CO, through appropriate channels, copy
furnished the local sanggunian concerned and Regional Offices of the DBM and
DILG; (b) instruct the School Board Secretary, to monitor and ensure the submission
and posting of the SEF Annual Budget and SEF Utilization Reports, both quarterly
and annually, pursuant to Item 6.1 of the DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s.
2017; and (c) instruct the Municipal Accountant to coordinate with the Municipal
Budget Office to ensure that accurate SEF Utilization Reports are prepared and
posted.

10.9. We recommended that the Local Chief Executive (a) continually comply with
the GAD mechanisms and processes to sustain the LGU’s critical consciousness in
supporting gender and development; and (b) require the GAD Focal Person to
comply with the provisions of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum
Circular No. 2013-01 and COA Circular No. 2014-001 particularly on the conduct of
gender audit and gender mainstreaming in the Municipality.

11.7. We recommended and the management agreed to a) assess its existing
organizational structure so that the ideal number of plantilla personnel will be
established, thereby, avoiding the hiring of an excessive number of job order
personnel; b) that the hiring of job order personnel be resorted to based on necessity in
order to avoid waste of much needed funds, as prescribed in Section 76 of RA 7160; and
c) strictly adhere to the policies and guidelines in the hiring of personnel under job order as
provided in CSC Memorandum No. 17, s. 2002 promulgating CSC Resolution No. 02-0790 dated June 5, 2002.

11.8. We also recommended and the HRMO agreed thru the Sangguniang Bayan to create a
written policy or manual governing the recruitment of job order and casual personnel.

12.6. We recommended and the OIC Municipal Treasurer agreed to continue the
withholding and remittance of contributions and repayments of loan amortizations
deducted from salaries of regular/permanent employees within the prescribed period
in adherence to Section 6 (b) of Republic Act No. 8291.

12.7. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to exert efforts
to trace and reconcile unremitted prior years’ balances.

12.8 Likewise, the Municipal Accountant also explained that the unremitted balance has
been existing in the books since prior to his assumption of office in 2015, but efforts are
being exerted to trace the origin of the said balance, although great difficulty is encountered
due to unavailability of related documents especially those located at the old municipal
building.

13.5. We recommended and the (OIC) Municipal Treasurer agreed to continue the
remittance of contributions and repayments of loan amortizations deducted from
salaries of regular/permanent employees and job orders within the prescribed period
in accordance with the provision of RA No. 9679.

13.6. We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to (a) exert best
efforts to reconcile employees’ records with the HDMF to be able to trace the affected
employees on the unremitted accumulated balance of the Due to Pag-IBIG account,
if any; (b) check with the records whether all Pag-IBIG premiums, Multi-Purpose
Loan repayments and Housing Loan repayments deducted from the payroll of
employees and Government Share (GS) for all employees were remitted; and (c) cause
the immediate remittance to Pag-IBIG of the unremitted premiums and contributions
of employees, as warranted by the result of inquiry and reconciliation with HDMF.

14.7. We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the
Municipal Accountant and Municipal Treasurer to (a) continue to comply with the
deduction of Philhealth contributions of its employees; (b) promptly remit all
deductions made to Philhealth pursuant to Section 18(b) of RA No. 7875 and Its
Implementing Rules and Regulations, respectively; and (c) exert efforts to trace and
reconcile unremitted prior years’ balance.

14.8. Likewise, the Municipal Accountant also explained that the unremitted balance has
been existing in the books since prior to his assumption of office in 2015, but efforts are
being exerted to trace the origin of the said balance, although great difficulty is encountered
due to unavailability of related documents especially those located at the old municipal
building.

15.4. We recommended and Management agreed to continue to withhold taxes and
check that all taxes withheld are remitted intact and immediately to BIR within the
prescribed period as required by Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 23-2007 dated
March 23, 2007.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor initiate the filing of a
request for write-off of receivables aged 10 years or more wherein settlement/
collectability is remote.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Treasurer, together with the Municipal Social Welfare and Development Officer
(MSWDO), to constitute appropriate collection strategies to recover the past due
receivable accounts to ensure cash availability for future beneficiaries and
continuity of the loan programs.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor instruct the:

a. OIC-Municipal Assessor to facilitate the titling of all the parcels of
land in the name of the Municipality to establish ownership and
ensure control over the properties and to avoid any legal impediments
due to possible third-party claims; and
b. Municipal Accountant to submit the required lacking documents and
ensure that payments of claims for the purchases of land are
supported with complete supporting documents.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the Municipal
Accountant to revisit the policy on the estimation of the useful life and residual
value of the Municipalitys PPE for a reliable and fair presentation of the
financial statements of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor refrain from hiring a legal
consultant to avoid payments of legal fees out of public funds. The Municipality
may instead seek the assistance of the Provincial Legal Officer or Provincial
Prosecutor in handling the legal affairs of the Municipality.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all the
required supporting documents are attached to the DVs and payrolls before
processing claims for payments, to establish the regularity, validity and
propriety of the transactions.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor require the:

a. BAC to: (i) strictly comply with the provisions of the updated 2016
Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 regarding the completeness and
propriety of procurement documents, invitation to observers, and
posting requirements, among others, to enhance transparency and
establish the regularity of the procurement process; and (ii) conduct
competitive bidding in all its procurement and resort only to
alternative methods of procurement whenever justified by the
conditions provided in the same Revised IRR; and
b. Municipal Accountant to check the completeness and propriety of
supporting documents prescribed under Sections 9.1 and 9.2 of COA
Circular No. 2012-001 before processing claims for payments to
ensure compliance with 2016 Revised IRR of RA No. 9184 and to
establish the legality, validity, and propriety of the disbursements.

We recommended and management agreed to the following:
a. The Municipal Assessor assist the Municipal Engineer in the assessment of the fair value of the road lot component of local roads under the jurisdiction of the Municipal Government; and
b. The Municipal Accountant recognize the amount pertaining to the road lot as part of the recorded book value of the road network accounts based on the appraised value provided by the Municipal Assessor.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to reconcile the RPT and SET Receivables with the list of tax delinquencies as of December 31, 2023 and make the necessary adjustments to present fairly the financial statements. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant take extra care in the recording of the RPT and SET Receivable for the current year using the list of taxpayers and amount due and collectible provided by the Municipal Treasurer.
observations.reason: Records are still being reconciled to establish the correct delinquent accounts.
Reiterated in Observation Nos. 12, 13, 14 and 15 of this report.

In addition, we recommended that Management transfer the unexpended DRRMF-MOOE and QRF to the depository account for Trust Liability-DRRM fund. Henceforth, stop the practice of disbursing expenditures under the LDRRM-Trust Liabilities from the General Fund.
observations.reason: Reiterated in observation no. 9 of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Commission on Audit all financial reports and accounts within the time prescribed by regulations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer to formulate strategies to intensify and facilitate tax collections. Avail of the remedies embodied in Section 174 of RA 7160 to ensure collection of taxes due the municipal government in order to increase the delivery of basic services.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
a. Management submit list of all on-going government projects/ programs/ activities (PPAs) at the beginning of the year and those that are to be implemented during the year; and
b. the Municipal Engineer inform our office within ten (10) days after the award of the infrastructure projects on or before the start of the PPAs that the appropriate projects signboards and /or public notices are already posted at the project site, for proper validation by this Office.

We recommended and Management agreed to require contractors to submit required certifications from the Insurance Commission to ensure that the insurance company are duly authorized by the Insurance Commission to issue such securities and issued surety bonds are valid and callable upon demand.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following:
a. Management submit Approved Technical Specifications and ABC for the procurement of goods under review, if any, for audit/evaluation; and
b. Management submit lacking documents listed in Item 14.10. Henceforth, BAC see to it that documents required in Sec. 37.2.3 of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 form part of the contract.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following actions be undertaken by Management:
 a. The Municipal Local Development Council clearly indicate in the AIP the implementation schedules of PPAs and evaluate the unimplemented PPAs whether these are still priority concerns and implement or re-appropriate them accordingly;
 b. The Bids and Awards Committee to strictly follow the procurement schedule and procure infrastructure projects with longer contract duration in the first quarter or semester of the year; and
 c. The Municipal Engineering Office to closely supervise the implementation of infrastructure projects and coordinate with other concerned government offices to ensure completion of projects on time.
We recommended and Management agreed to submit Monthly Report on the Sources and Utilization of the DRRMF on or before 15th day after the end of each month to the audit team.
observations.reason: Monthly reports have been submitted but not within the required timeframe.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that Management optimize the utilization of its LDRRMF to lessen the socio-economic and environmental impacts of disasters in the community and disaster risks, and to enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities of the community.

observations.recommendation: We recommended and Management agreed to strictly utilize the LDRRMF for implementation of DRRM Programs and regular Maintenance and Other Operating Expenses are to be sourced from other funds.

No. 10121 and Items 5.1.10 and 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated 12 September 2012 to strengthen the capacity of the National Government and the LGUs for disaster risk reduction and management.

observations.recommendation: We commended the Management for their effort to lessen the wastes of the Municipality and lessening the cost incurred for its disposal. However, we also recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board, through the SWM Focal Person, to: (a) establish MRF in the 11 barangays if possible or support the barangay government of the remaining 11 barangays that have no MRF, to facilitate the segregation of solid waste at the barangay level; (b) coordinate and require all barangays located in the coastal areas to have proper solid waste management disposal and closely monitor their implementation; and (c) instruct the barangay officials to inform their barangay collectors/segregators/ sorters to properly sort the wastes they had collected before transferring them to the Central MRF.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the above noted lapses, we recommended the following:
a. The Municipal Assessor assist the Municipal Engineer in the
assessment of the fair value of the road lot component of local roads
under the jurisdiction of the Municipal Government; and
b. The Municipal Accountant recognize the amount pertaining to the road
lot as part of the recorded book value of the road network accounts based
on the appraised value provided by the Municipal Assessor.

We recommended that Management determine the details of the recorded
Land in the books. Also, we recommended that the Management exhaust all
possible means to document the legality of ownership of the lots through a
Transfer Certificate of Title (TCT) in the name of the LGU.

We recommended that Management direct the Municipal Accountant to
reconcile the RPT and SET Receivables with the list of tax delinquencies as
of December 31, 2023 and make the necessary adjustments to present fairly
the financial statements. Henceforth, the Municipal Accountant take extra
care in the recording of the RPT and SET Receivable for the current year
using the list of taxpayers and amount due and collectible provided by the
Municipal Treasurer.

We recommended and Management agreed to provide the audit team copy
of the memorandum of agreement for the implementation of the OTOP
Project and communicate with the Office of the President if said project is
still effective for implementation. If not, the amount of ₱1,000,000.00 should
be fully refunded to the said office or returned to the National Treasury.

In view of the above noted lapses, we recommended and Management
agreed to the following:
a. The Municipal Treasurer immediately remit the amount of ₱1,108.70 to
the office of the PAG-IBIG; and
b. The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer trace back
transactions to identify the Municipal Officials and Employees that the
prior year withholdings amounting to ₱3,957.08 for Due to GSIS and
₱8,543.56 for Due to Pag-ibig were due, and remit the said premiums/
loans or consider reconciling their records with the office of the GSIS and
PAG-IBIG;

We recommended that the Municipal accountant submit subsidiary ledgers
stated in item 6.2, if any. Henceforth, Municipal accountant maintain required
subsidiary ledgers to establish accuracy of the balances provided in the
financial statements.

In addition, we recommended that Management transfer the unexpended
DRRMF-MOOE and QRF to the depository account for Trust Liability-DRRM
fund. Henceforth, stop the practice of disbursing expenditures under the
LDRRM-Trust Liabilities from the General Fund.

We recommended and Management agreed to require the Municipal
Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to submit to the Commission on Audit
all financial reports and accounts within the time prescribed by regulations.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the Municipal Treasurer
to formulate strategies to intensify and facilitate tax collections. Avail of the
remedies embodied in Section 174 of RA 7160 to ensure collection of taxes due
the municipal government in order to increase the delivery of basic services.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Municipal Treasurer
to collect building permit fees with the assistance of the Municipal Engineering
Office and dispose such fees in compliance with Section 208 of IRR of the
National Building Code of the Philippines and DPWH and DILG JMC No. 001 to
ensure the quality of buildings and other structures as well as the safety of its
constituents and to generate additional income to support LGU’s basic
services.

In view of the above noted lapses, we recommended and Management
agreed to the following:
a. Management submit list of all on-going government projects/
programs/ activities (PPAs) at the beginning of the year and those that
are to be implemented during the year; and
b. the Municipal Engineer inform our office within ten (10) days after the
award of the Infrastructure projects on or before the start of the PPAs
that the appropriate projects signboards and /or public notices are
already posted at the project site, for proper validation by this Office.

We recommended and Management agreed to require contractors to submit
required certifications from the Insurance Commission to ensure that the
insurance company are duly authorized by the Insurance Commission to
issue such securities and issued surety bonds are valid and callable upon
demand.

We recommended and Management agreed to direct the Sanggunian to
conduct the required public hearing for the adoption and approval of the
CLUP.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit contract documents
within five working days from execution in compliance with COA Circular No.
2009-001 for early detection and correction of errors/deficiencies.

In view of the above noted lapses, we recommended and Management
agreed to the following:

a. Management submit Approved Technical Specifications and ABC for the
procurement of goods under review, if any, for audit/evaluation; and
b. Management submit lacking documents listed in Item 14.10. Henceforth,
BAC see to it that documents required in Sec. 37.2.3 of the Revised IRR
of R.A. No. 9184 form part of the contract.

We recommended and Management agreed to the following actions be
undertaken by Management:

a. The Municipal Local Development Council clearly indicate in the AIP the
implementation schedules of PPAs and evaluate the unimplemented
PPAs whether these are still priority concerns and implement or re-
appropriate them accordingly;
b. The Bids and Awards Committee to strictly follow the procurement
schedule and procure infrastructure projects with longer contract
duration in the first quarter or semester of the year; and
c. The Municipal Engineering Office to closely supervise the
implementation of infrastructure projects and coordinate with other
concerned government offices to ensure completion of projects on
time.

We recommended and Management agreed to submit Monthly Report on the
Sources and Utilization of the DRRMF on or before 15th day after the end of
each month to the audit team.

We recommended that Management optimize the utilization of its LDRRMF
to lessen the socio-economic and environmental impacts of disasters in the
community and disaster risks, and to enhance disaster preparedness and
response capabilities of the community.

However, we still recommended and that they agreed to continue complying Section 21 of R.A. No. 10121 and Items 5.1.10 and 5.1.13 of COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated 12 September 2012 to strengthen the capacity of the National Government and the LGUs for disaster risk reduction and management.

However, we also recommended that the Local Chief Executive instruct the (a) LDRRM Officer to:
include under a separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged to the unexpended LDRRMF of previous years in the LDRRMIFP; and
continue utilizing the fund for the implementation of projects and activities for disaster prevention and mitigation, preparedness, response and rehabilitation and recovery.

However, we still recommended and the Local Chief Executive, Sangguniang Bayan and Local Development Council agreed to continue complying with the above regulations and to continue:
(a) investing in high-impact capital expenditure projects in the utilization of its 20% Development Fund; and
(b) ensuring the effective and efficient implementation of the proposed projects within their targeted completion period through close monitoring and supervision to maximize the developmental benefits and the early enjoyment thereof by the intended beneficiaries.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to:
(a) continually comply with the GAD mechanisms and processes to sustain the LGU’s critical consciousness in supporting gender and development thru women’s empowerment; and
(b) require the GAD Focal Person to comply with the provisions of PCW, DILG, DBM and NEDA Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2013-01 and COA Circular No. 2014-001 particularly on the conduct of gender audit and gender mainstreaming in the Municipality.

However, we also recommended and the Management agreed that the Municipal Solid Waste Management Board, through the SWM Focal Person, to:
(a) 	establish MRF in the 11 barangays if possible or support the barangay government of the remaining 11 barangays that have no MRF, to facilitate the segregation of solid waste at the barangay level;
(b) 	coordinate and require all barangays located in the coastal areas to have proper solid waste management disposal and closely monitor their implementation; and
(c) 	instruct the barangay officials to inform their barangay collectors/segregators/ sorters to properly sort the wastes they had collected before transferring them to the Central MRF.

We recommended and the General Services Office agreed to procure, as far as practicable and feasible, directly from the PS-DBM all anticipated common-use supplies, and resort to local suppliers only upon unavailability of supplies needed using the prescribed alternative modes of procurement.

We recommend and the Acting Municipal Treasurer agreed to continue the withholding and remittance of contributions and repayments of loan amortizations deducted from salaries of regular/permanent employees within the prescribed period in adherence to Section 6 (b) of Republic Act No. 8291.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to exert efforts to trace and reconcile unremitted prior years’ balance.

We recommended and the (OIC) Municipal Treasurer agreed to continue the remittance of contributions and repayments of loan amortizations deducted from salaries of regular/permanent employees and job orders within the prescribed period in accordance with the provisions of RA No. 9679.

We also recommended and the Municipal Accountant agreed to:
(a) exert best efforts to reconcile employees’ records with the HDMF to be able to trace the affected employees on the unremitted accumulated balance of the Due to Pag-IBIG account, if any;
(b) check with the records whether all Pag-IBIG premiums, Multi-Purpose Loan repayments and Housing Loan repayments deducted from the payroll of employees and Government Share (GS) for all employees were remitted; and
(c) cause the immediate remittance to Pag-IBIG of the unremitted premiums and contributions of employees, as warranted by the result of inquiry and reconciliation with HDMF.

We recommended and the Local Chief Executive agreed to instruct the Municipal Accountant and (OIC) Municipal Treasurer to (a) continue to comply with the deduction of PhilHealth contributions of its employees; (b) promptly remit all deductions made to pursuant to Section 18(b) of RA No. 7875 and Its Implementing Rules and Regulations, respectively; and (c) exert efforts to trace and reconcile unremitted prior years’ balance.

We recommended and the Management agreed to (a) continue to withhold taxes and check that all taxes withheld are remitted intact and immediately to BIR within the prescribed period as required by Revenue Memorandum Circular No. 23-2007 dated March 23, 2007.

We have also recommended that purchases beyond the threshold on shopping and small value items be done through public bidding as mandated under R.A. No. 9184 and its IRR.  Alternative methods of procurement shall be resorted to only in the highly exceptional cases provided for in R.A. No. 9184.  In all instances, the procuring entity shall ensure that the most advantageous price for the LGU is obtained.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation:
In view of the foregoing, we have recommended that:

The BAC members, BAC secretariat and TWG update and familiarize themselves with different rules and regulations on procurements as set forth by competent authority;
Management use DPWH Department Order No. 72, series of 2016 or its updated issuances on the matter in its succeeding computation of the ABC of infrastructure projects for CY 2017 onwards; and
Justify the above noted lapses.
observations.reason: Infrastructure projects implemented by administration including the contract for the “Newly Opened Poblacion – Gayaman FMR” were not supported with ABC.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management justify/explain the release of advance payment/ 15% mobilization fund to the contractor without requiring the latter to post valid security equivalent to the amount released.  Henceforth, Management follow strictly the provisions of Items 4 and 5, Annex “E” of the Revised IRR of R.A. No. 9184 on advance and progress payments before the disbursement of the said payments.

We have recommended that management, in cases of lump sum appropriations containing general terms, seek a separate SB appropriation ordinance/resolution to serve as an authority to enter into contract in behalf of the Municipal Government in accordance with Section 22 (c) of R.A. No. 7160 and the applicable jurisprudence.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management justify the above-noted lapses and ensure that utilization of the 20% Development Fund be limited on programs, projects and activities that will contribute to the attainment of socio-economic development and environmental management outcomes and partake the nature of investment or capital expenditures that will yield greater benefit to the people in accordance with DBM-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2011-1.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management immediately submit the above-enumerated documents for proper review and evaluation of the said projects funded under the 20% Development Fund.

We have recommended that the Municipal officials implement their Local Disaster Risk Reduction Management Plan (LDRRMP) as mandated by law to lessen the socio-economic and environmental impacts of disaster in the community and disaster risks, including projected climate risks, and to enhance disaster preparedness and response capabilities of the community.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management prepare the MDRRMFIP for the Trust Liability – DRRMF in the ensuing years.
observations.reason: No expenditure was made for CY 2017 charged to the unexpended LDRRMF in the previous year/s.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended to Management that in cases of lump sum appropriations containing general terms, a separate SB appropriation ordinance/resolution must always be secured by the Municipal Mayor to serve as an authority to enter into contract in behalf of the Municipal Government in accordance with Section 22 (c) of R.A. No. 7160 and the applicable jurisprudence.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management comply with the regulations on the transfer of unexpended/unobligated balance of the LDRRMF to the Trust Fund.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that:

the utilization/liquidation reports with complete supporting documents be submitted for proper review and evaluation of the said projects funded under SEF; and
the prescribed rules and regulations on processing of disbursements be strictly observed.   Henceforth, ensure that all disbursements are properly obligated and supported with complete documentation before payments are made.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation:
We have recommended that the municipality ensure the alignment of the municipal GAD PPAs with the priorities of the province.  Henceforth, management shall see to it that its GPB be submitted to the PPDO of the Provincial Government, then from PPDO to the DILG Regional Office for review and approval, and a copy of which shall be furnished to COA within the reglementary period as prescribed under COA Circular No. 2014-001.

We have also recommended that the LGU establish its GFPS to ensure that GAD mechanisms and processes are in place and the creation of GAD M&E Team who shall evaluate the outcomes of the LGU GAD policies, programs and projects in accordance with Item 5.1 of the PCW-DILG-DBM-NEDA JMC No. 2013-01, as amended.  Management shall establish and/or strengthen their monitoring and evaluation systems to assess the effectiveness and efficiency of their gender mainstreaming efforts, and the M&E activities shall be included in the annual GPB.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation: We have reiterated our previous recommendation that the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer observe, at all times, prudence in withholding and filing of tax returns. The remittances on income taxes withheld should be compared with individual deductions of municipal officials and employees.  Summary of taxes withheld from suppliers/contractors should be prepared at the end of each month.  The remittances on VAT and EWT withheld should be compared with the individual deductions of suppliers/contractors.  We have also recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit the JEV and its supporting documents in order to establish the propriety and validity of the recorded adjusting entry.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that the Municipal Accountant and the Property Officer should see to it that all PPE were properly supported with updated ARE/MR.  AREs/MRs should be renewed every three years.  If not, these should not be considered in the physical count of PPE in accordance with Section 492 of GAAM Vol. 1.

We have recommend that the LGU Officials with the aid of the Municipal Assessor (for the land) and the Property Officer (for the Motor Vehicles/Construction and Heavy Equipment) obtain the certificate of title/certificate of registration in the name of the Municipality of Luba to prove ownership over the parcels of land and equipment acquired.
observations.managementAction: Management is currently in the process of obtaining land titles.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management should invite a representative of the agency auditor to serve as witness in the actual conduct of physical count on or before June 30 and December 31 of year and submit report of physical count to the agency auditor not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year.
observations.reason: Reiterated in this report.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that Management should allocate fund for the purchase of equipment and consider buying new equipment instead of spending excessive/uneconomic repair of old equipment.

We have recommended that the guidelines set to process financial assistance should be observed to regulate the utilization of the fund.  The amount set for each type of financial assistance should be complied with to ascertain a non-discretionary determination of amounts given to each client.  We have also recommended the Municipal Accountant to submit the noted lacking supporting documents in order to establish the validity of claims. The Municipal Accountant being responsible to the completeness of the supporting documents should not certify the disbursements unless all necessary documents are attached.
observations.reason: Guideline is not yet set to ascertain a non-discretionary determination of amounts given to each client.

observations.recommendation: We have recommended that management conduct technical study before entering into purchase agreement of second-hand vehicle. Also, management should provide the original documents showing the acquisition cost at which the vehicle was originally purchased as a brand new unit together with the computation of its RUV.
observations.managementAction: Management has been very careful in purchasing the said motor vehicle.  The mechanic together with the Municipal Engineer and the DRRMO-Designate did not only canvassed the said motor vehicle but they also did a thorough inspection and interview of the previous owner and of other vehicle-owners of that type.  As per their judgment, the motor vehicle purchased is of good running condition and reasonable price.

observations.managementAction: By virtue of Republic Act No. 7160, Share from Tobacco-producing LGUs are entitled to a 15% Excise Taxes on locally-manufactured Virginia-type cigarette LGU. Given that, the municipality had been actually realizing its share annually and regularly reported as income of the LGU. Consequently, the management treats its share from Virginia Tobacco Excise Tax as a regular income. The Municipal Accountant prepared to comply with the said requirements and obtain the necessary documents.

observations.managementAction:
Management has been very careful in purchasing the said motor vehicle. The mechanic together with the Municipal Engineer and the DRRMO-Designate did not only canvassed previous owner and other vehicle-owners of that type. As per their judgment, the motor vehicle purchased is of good running condition and reasonable price. Management should have considered mileage and the remaining useful life of the vehicle prior to its purchase. A technical study should have conducted showing, among others, certification/documentation on the general obsolescence of the vehicle, the availability of spare parts, and the quality, degree and extent of repair and maintenance requirements must also be considered in the acquisition of second-hand/reconditioned equipment. Further, Management should provide the original documents showing the acquisition cost at which the vehicle was originally purchased as a brand new unit together with the computation of its RUV to determine the reasonableness of the price.
For CY 2016, The municipality made disbursement amounting to ₱274,392.00 for refurbishment of rescue vehicle. Interview with agency personnel disclosed that the said disbursement was for the repair of the second-hand Mitsubishi Strada, subject of this observation.
Management purchased 4x4 mini dump truck (4HG1) (reconditioned) costing ₱1,170,212.77, but such procurement was not again supported with the above-stated study before entering into purchase agreement.
